title: Mucus thickness to prevent diseases
abstract: The present invention generally relates to the use of specific probiotic lactic acid bacterial strains for their capability of increasing the firmly adherent mucus layer thickness in the gastrointestinal tract of animals, including humans.
claims: Claims Claims 1. A lactic acid bacterial strain for use in increasing the thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer in the gastrointestinal tract of an animal. 2. The strain for use of claim 1 , wherein said strain does not have the capability to increase the thickness of the loosely adherent mucus layer in the gastrointestinal tract of said animal. 3. The strain for use of claim 1 or claim 2, wherein said use is in the treatment or prevention of a gastrointestinal disease associated with reduced thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer. 4. The strain for use of claim 3, wherein said gastrointestinal disease is a disease associated with a lost or thin firmly adherent mucus layer. 5. The strain for use of any one of claims 1 to 4, wherein said use is in the treatment or prevention of a disease associated with abnormal intestinal permeability. 6. The strain for use of any one of claims 1 to 5, wherein said gastrointestinal disease is a disease selected from the group consisting of colitis, Crohn's disease, Inflammatory Bowel Disease, Irritable Bowel Syndrome, diverticulosis, stress induced gastrointestinal conditions and infections of the gastrointestinal tract caused by pathogenic organisms, preferably wherein said gastrointestinal disease is ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease. 7. The strain for use of any one of claims 3 to 6, wherein said disease is an early stage gastrointestinal disease. 8. The strain for use of claim 3, wherein said use is in the prevention of a gastrointestinal disease and said animal is a healthy animal. 9. The strain for use of claim 8, wherein said animal is at risk of developing diseases of the gastrointestinal tract or suffering from conditions of the gastrointestinal tract, preferably wherein said animal has a family history of gastrointestinal disease, genetic predisposition to gastrointestinal disease, an allergy or intolerance to certain food-types, psychological problems, a stressful job or lifestyle, or has been prescribed with or is being subjected to pharmaceutical treatments which have gastrointestinal problems as a potential side effect. 10. The strain for use of any one of claims 1 to 9, wherein said strain is Lactobacillus reuteri, preferably wherein said strain is Lactobacillus reuteri ATCC PTA 4659. 1 1. The strain for use of any one of claims 1 to 10, wherein said animal is a human. 12. A method of selecting a probiotic lactic acid bacterial strain with the capability to increase the thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer in the gastrointestinal tract of an animal, wherein said method comprises screening a lactic acid bacterial strain for the capability to increase the thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer in an animal and selecting a strain which shows such capability, optionally wherein said method further comprises screening said lactic acid bacterial strain for the capability not to increase the thickness of the loosely adherent mucus layer in the gastrointestinal tract in an animal and selecting a strain which shows such capability. 13. The method of claim 12, wherein said screening step to measure the capability to increase the thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer and/or the capability not to increase the thickness of the loosely adherent mucus layer is carried out using an in vivo assay, preferably in a mouse. 14. The method of claim 12 or claim 13, wherein the capability of the strains to increase the thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer in an animal is assessed on a sample in which the thickness of the firmly adherent mucus layer is normal and/or on a sample where the firmly adherent mucus layer is thinner than normal. 15. The method of any one of claims 12 to 14, wherein said strain to be assessed in the selection methods is a strain of Lactobacillus, more preferably Lactobacillus reuteri.
